prior.content_md:
*Note: This section covers the requirements that apply to an organisation electing to report in accordance with the GRI Standards. These are not statutory requirements. They are the internal requirements of the framework.*

The source content retrieved is the standards listing page, not the full text of individual standards. The following describes the confirmed architecture of requirements, with specific disclosure content noted as a research gap where the full standard text was not retrieved.

## Universal Standards (GRI 1, 2, 3) — Applies to all GRI reporters

GRI 1: Foundation 2021 establishes the purpose, system, and concepts of GRI reporting. GRI 2: General Disclosures 2021 covers organisational disclosures on governance, strategy, policies, and practices. GRI 3: Material Topics 2021 governs the process of determining which topics are material to the organisation's reporting.

For a freight forwarder, the materiality process under GRI 3 would need to determine which of the topic standards — emissions, energy, climate change, labour practices, supply chain — are material given the organisation's impacts across its cargo verticals and transport modes.

**Research gap:** The specific disclosure line items within GRI 2 and GRI 3 as revised in 2021 require review of the full standard text, not confirmed from the source content retrieved.

*Source: GRI Standards English Language listing, Global Reporting Initiative (GRI 1, 2, 3 all confirmed published 05 Oct 2021). https://www.globalreporting.org/how-to-use-the-gri-standards/gri-standards-english-language/*

## GRI 305: Emissions 2016 — Highest operational materiality for freight

GRI 305 covers emissions disclosures. For an air-primary freight forwarder, Scope 1, Scope 2, and Scope 3 emissions disclosures — including emissions from transport operations — are the core of this standard's application.

**Research gap:** The specific disclosure items within GRI 305 (e.g., whether Scope 3 category 4 upstream transport and distribution is explicitly required) require review of the full GRI 305 standard text, which was not retrieved.

*Source: GRI 305: Emissions 2016 confirmed published, Global Reporting Initiative. https://www.globalreporting.org/how-to-use-the-gri-standards/gri-standards-english-language/*

## GRI 102: Climate Change 2025 and GRI 103: Energy 2025 — Most recently updated, highest relevance

Both were published 26 June 2025, making them the most current standards on the topics most directly relevant to freight operations. These supersede or supplement prior treatment of climate and energy topics within the GRI framework.

**Research gap:** The specific disclosure requirements introduced or revised in GRI 102: 2025 and GRI 103: 2025 are not confirmed from the source content retrieved. The full standard texts must be obtained from the GRI download page to assess operational impact.

*Source: GRI 102: Climate Change 2025 and GRI 103: Energy 2025 confirmed published 26 Jun 2025, Global Reporting Initiative. https://www.globalreporting.org/how-to-use-the-gri-standards/gri-standards-english-language/*

## GRI 308: Supplier Environmental Assessment 2016 — Applies to the workspace as a supply chain subject

GRI 308 governs how reporting organisations assess the environmental impacts of their suppliers. The workspace, as a freight forwarder and tier-1 service provider to large corporate clients who may be GRI reporters, sits in the subject position of this standard.

**Research gap:** The specific data fields and assessment criteria GRI 308 calls for from suppliers require review of the full GRI 308 standard text.

*Source: GRI 308: Supplier Environmental Assessment 2016 confirmed published, Global Reporting Initiative. https://www.globalreporting.org/how-to-use-the-gri-standards/gri-standards-english-language/*
